questions.stem: In the basic code-generation feedback loop (as in "Synthesize, Execute & Debug"), what is supplied back to the LLM when the generated code fails its unit tests?
options: The runtime error messages and failing-test output, together with the previously generated code
Only the original problem description, so the LLM starts from scratch
A human-written patch that the LLM must merge into the code
The execution traces of the tests that passed, to reinforce correct behavior
questions.explanation: The debugger agent collects the runtime errors and failed-test output and feeds them, along with the earlier code, to the LLM to regenerate a fix. Restarting from only the description would discard the diagnostic signal the loop is built around.

questions.stem: According to the lecture, agent self-reflection is best defined as:
options: Fine-tuning the agent's model on a log of its past failed actions
Comparing the agent's answer against answers produced by other agents
A generation task where an agent reviews the results of its actions and tries to identify why they did not achieve the desired result
Re-running the same prompt at a higher temperature to obtain a different action
questions.explanation: Self-reflection is a special generation task: the agent examines its own action's outcome and verbalizes why it failed. It requires no weight updates and no other agents — those options describe fine-tuning and debate instead.

questions.stem: Compared with simply feeding raw error messages back to the programmer agent, what step does the Reflexion approach add to the code-generation loop?
options: It executes the tests a second time in a sandbox to filter out flaky failures
An agent reflects on the errors and verbalizes what went wrong, and this insight is given to the programmer agent for regeneration
It generates additional unit tests to increase coverage before debugging
It fine-tunes the debugger model on the collected error logs
questions.explanation: Reflexion inserts a reflection agent that analyzes the error messages and verbalizes the underlying problem; this richer feedback (rather than raw errors) improves the chance of regenerating correct code. Test generation is the AgentCoder addition, not Reflexion's.

questions.stem: Beyond the failing program and its error messages, the more elaborate reflection prompt shown in class adds which two elements?
options: The unit-test suite and a line-coverage report
A chain-of-thought exemplar and an explicit temperature instruction
The developer's commit history and the project style guide
The original problem description and a bug category derived from pre-processing the error messages
questions.explanation: The elaborate prompt includes the original problem description used to generate the code plus a bug category obtained by pre-processing and categorizing the errors, giving the LLM more context for a precise diagnosis.

questions.stem: Which sequence best describes AgentCoder's code-synthesis workflow?
options: Generate code, generate unit tests for it, execute those tests on the code, and regenerate the code if tests fail
Generate code, compare it against coding best practices, and rewrite any non-conforming parts
Generate code, run only the unit tests supplied by the user, and debug failures
Generate several code candidates in parallel and let a judge agent vote for the best one
questions.explanation: AgentCoder's distinguishing feature is that a test designer agent generates its own unit tests, a test executor runs them, and failures drive code regeneration. Relying solely on user-provided tests misses the test-generation agent that gives the system its power.

questions.stem: AgentCoder's Test Designer Agent prompts the LLM to create test cases satisfying which three criteria?
options: Basic test cases, edge test cases, and large-scale inputs for performance and scalability
Unit tests, integration tests, and end-to-end tests
Positive tests, negative tests, and randomized fuzzing inputs
Fast tests, deterministic tests, and mutation-resistant tests
questions.explanation: The Test Designer is instructed to generate basic test cases, cover edge cases, and cover large-scale inputs to assess performance and scalability. The other triads are real testing taxonomies but not AgentCoder's prompt criteria.

questions.stem: An AgentCoder-style test designer produces a test that FAILS on a program that is actually a correct implementation of the task. In the lecture's terminology, this generated test is a:
options: False positive
Flaky test
False negative
True negative
questions.explanation: A false negative is when the generated test says the program is incorrect even though it is really correct. A false positive is the reverse — the test blesses an incorrect program.

questions.stem: Generated unit tests can err in two directions. According to the lecture, which outcome poses the more serious risk once the delivered code is actually used?
options: A false negative, because rejecting correct code wastes regeneration cycles and compute
Both are equally serious since each misleads the programmer agent the same way
Neither matters once self-consistency validation has been applied
A false positive, because an incorrect implementation is accepted and can produce faulty results when the code runs
questions.explanation: The lecture notes that rejecting a correct implementation (false negative) is 'not so bad,' but accepting an incorrect one (false positive) can lead to faulty results in operation. Self-consistency reduces, but never eliminates, incorrect tests.

questions.stem: How did the AgentCoder authors measure whether the tests produced by the test designer agent were accurate?
options: They asked human developers to review a random sample of the generated tests
They ran the generated tests on the ground-truth program and deemed a test correct if the ground-truth program passed it
They checked whether the generated tests achieved full branch coverage on the generated code
They compared each generated test against the benchmark's official test suite for exact matches
questions.explanation: Accuracy was measured by executing the generated tests against the known-correct ground-truth implementation: a test is correct if the ground truth passes it. By this measure the designer agent was almost 90% accurate.

questions.stem: In the AgentCoder ablation study, the "programmer + test designer" configuration differs from the full three-agent system mainly in that it:
options: Never executes any code — the programmer only analyzes the generated tests and decides whether to modify the code
Executes only the benchmark-provided tests instead of the generated ones
Skips test generation and relies on the programmer's self-review
Uses a separate judge agent to select among candidate programs
questions.explanation: In that configuration the generated code and tests are fed back to the programmer to reason about, but there is no code execution and thus no execution feedback. Using only prompt-supplied tests with execution describes the 'programmer + test executor' variant instead.

questions.stem: Per the AgentCoder authors, why can having the SAME agent generate both the code and its unit tests hurt performance?
options: The combined prompt exceeds the model's context window, truncating the tests
The agent alternates between the two tasks indefinitely and never terminates
The tests can be biased by flaws in the agent's own code — e.g., if the code ignores edge cases, the tests inherit that flaw — and dual duties distract the agent's focus
Test generation requires a fine-tuned model while code generation does not
questions.explanation: The authors hypothesize that a single agent loses objectivity — its tests are biased by the code it wrote — and that juggling both tasks distracts its focus. Infinite alternation between tasks is a tempting but unsupported reason.

questions.stem: Fine-tuning an LLM refers to:
options: Using LLM-generated unit tests to raise the model's accuracy on a programming domain
Adapting a pre-trained model by training it on a smaller, task-specific dataset
Having the agent analyze similar past requests to reduce its failure rate
Having agent A critique agent B's initial solution so B can improve it
questions.explanation: Fine-tuning takes a pre-trained foundational model and continues training it on a domain-specific dataset for specialized performance. Critique between agents is debate/reflection, not fine-tuning.

questions.stem: Which statement about the mechanics of fine-tuning is accurate?
options: It changes only the system prompt while the model stays frozen
It attaches an external retrieval index without touching the model
It retrains only the tokenizer so domain terms become single tokens
It updates the underlying weights of the model by training on new labeled data
questions.explanation: Fine-tuning changes the model's underlying weights via training on task-specific examples — unlike prompt engineering or RAG, which leave the weights untouched.

questions.stem: In fine-tuned unit-test generation, a generator with a HIGH attack rate is one whose tests:
options: Always predict the output that the correct implementation produces
Execute quickly enough to run on every regeneration cycle
Provide inputs likely to expose errors — an incorrect implementation g yields g(x) != y
Use few tokens so many tests fit in the debugging prompt
questions.explanation: Attack rate measures the ability to catch faulty code: for an incorrect implementation g of the task, the test input should make g produce the wrong output. Matching the correct implementation's output is the separate 'output accuracy' objective.

questions.stem: Research cited in class found that zero-shot models show a strong trade-off between attack rate and output accuracy in unit-test generation. What is the reason?
options: The inputs most likely to trigger errors are challenging edge cases, whose correct outputs are hardest for the model to predict
Both properties improve together with model size, so the trade-off only appears in small models
High-attack tests take longer to run, forcing the generator to sacrifice accuracy for speed
Accurate outputs require long reasoning chains that overflow the context window on edge cases
questions.explanation: Error-triggering inputs tend to be difficult edge cases, and precisely those inputs are the ones whose expected outputs the model most often gets wrong — so raising attack rate lowers output accuracy in zero-shot settings.

questions.stem: When building the fine-tuning dataset for a unit-test generator, a candidate input-output pair (x,y) for problem description d is accepted only if:
options: An LLM judge rates the pair as plausible for the description d
Both the ground-truth implementation f and the perturbed implementation g return y on x
The input x already appears somewhere in the Tulu-3 dataset
The ground-truth implementation satisfies f(x) = y while a buggy implementation g, created by perturbing f, gives g(x) != y
questions.explanation: Samples must exhibit both target properties: the correct code f passes the test (output accuracy) and a deliberately perturbed buggy version g fails it (attack rate). If both f and g agreed, the test could not discriminate correct from incorrect code.

questions.stem: How is self-consistency used to further validate a generated unit test (x,y) for problem description d?
options: The test is run on k different candidate programs and kept if most of them pass it
k different agents debate the test and a judge decides whether to keep it
The LLM is asked k times to produce the correct output for input x, and the test is kept only if a majority of the k answers produce y
The test is paraphrased k times and kept only if all paraphrases compile
questions.explanation: Self-consistency queries the LLM k times for the output on input x given d; the pair (x,y) is accepted as a valid test only if the majority of the k generations agree on y. Agreement across diverse reasoning paths raises confidence in the test's correctness.

questions.stem: Your generated code passes every unit test produced by a fine-tuned, self-consistency-validated test generator, and correctness is critical for your application. According to the lecture, you should:
options: Ship the code — this pipeline yields fully trustworthy tests
Still manually check the generated code, since even validated generated tests are never 100% correct
Re-run the same tests at temperature 0 to confirm the results deterministically
Add a multi-agent debate round, which guarantees any remaining bugs are found
questions.explanation: Even with fine-tuning and self-consistency, generated unit tests are not 100% correct, so when certainty is required you must still manually verify the code — like all LLM-generated artifacts. No technique in the lecture removes this need.

questions.stem: The lecture characterizes collaborative agent architectures along which three dimensions?
options: Communication model, control flow, and topology
Latency, throughput, and cost
Planning, memory, and tool use
Prompting strategy, model choice, and dataset
questions.explanation: The three dimensions are the communication model (how agents exchange information), control flow (who decides which agents run and how results combine), and topology (who can talk to whom).

questions.stem: In the shared-blackboard communication model:
options: Agents exchange information via direct peer-to-peer messages
Only the orchestrator may read what other agents have written
Agents share model weights that are updated after every step
Each agent writes its output to shared state, and every agent can see the entire history of agent outputs
questions.explanation: With a blackboard, all outputs go to a common shared state visible to every agent, giving each agent the full interaction history. Direct peer-to-peer exchange is the contrasting message-passing model.

questions.stem: You are building a multi-agent system that responds to incoming alerts, where each agent independently decides whether a given alert is relevant to it and reacts on its own. Which control-flow style fits best?
options: Orchestrated — a central agent assigns each alert to a worker
Chain — alerts flow through the agents in a fixed order
Hierarchical — a manager delegates each alert down a tree of workers
Autonomous — agents act independently, triggered by events or changes to global state
questions.explanation: When each agent self-selects work in reaction to events, autonomous control flow (agents triggered by events or global-state changes, e.g. via an event bus) is the natural fit; an orchestrator would centralize a decision the agents are meant to make themselves.

questions.stem: Orchestrated multi-agent models give clear control and are easier to refine toward desired results. Per the lecture, what is their main downside?
options: The orchestrator is a single point of failure and a potential bottleneck
Agents cannot be given specialized roles under an orchestrator
They require a shared blackboard, which leaks context between agents
The orchestrator can only invoke agents sequentially, never concurrently
questions.explanation: The trade-off for clear central control is that the orchestrator becomes a single point of failure and can bottleneck the system. Orchestrators can in fact invoke agents sequentially or concurrently, so that option is a misconception.

questions.stem: Which trade-off correctly characterizes a hierarchical multi-agent topology, where parents delegate tasks to children and communication is only between parent and child?
options: It maximizes information sharing but requires every agent to talk to every other agent
It is the easiest topology to debug but cannot contain more than two layers
It scales better than flat orchestration and is modular, but adds latency from multiple layers and increases coordination complexity
It removes any single point of failure but forces all agents to share one context window
questions.explanation: Hierarchies scale and modularize well because each parent manages only its children, but every extra layer adds latency and coordination overhead. Restricting communication to parent-child links is the opposite of everyone-talks-to-everyone.

questions.stem: In the CAMEL role-playing example (a stock trader agent and a Python programmer agent), what happens after the human describes the task and the roles?
options: An orchestrator decomposes the task and dispatches subtasks to both agents in parallel
The LLM refines the task with more detail, then the two role agents take turns executing and updating the collaboration state
The two agents debate competing designs until a judge selects the winner
Each agent independently produces a full solution and the human merges them
questions.explanation: In CAMEL the LLM first refines the human's task description, and then the role agents — the user agent specifying programming tasks and the programmer agent executing them — alternate turns, updating a shared collaboration state. There is no central orchestrator or judge.

questions.stem: In the Coder framework for resolving GitHub issues, a test that reproduces the issue already exists, and the system must now determine which code is responsible for the bug. Which agent role performs this step?
options: Fault Localizer
Reproducer
Editor
Verifier
questions.explanation: The Fault Localizer identifies the code responsible for the issue. The Reproducer generates the reproducing test, the Editor performs the code changes, and the Verifier runs integration tests to confirm the fix.

questions.stem: In the Coder framework, which agent role selects the plan for resolving the issue and determines whether the issue is solved and, if not, what to do next?
options: Verifier
Editor
Manager
Reproducer
questions.explanation: The Manager owns the control decisions: it picks the resolution plan and judges whether the issue is resolved or further steps are needed. The Verifier only runs integration tests to check that the issue is fixed.

questions.stem: What is the core idea of Multi-Agent Debate (MAD)?
options: Multiple agents split a task into subtasks and solve them independently in parallel
One agent generates a solution and a second agent silently rewrites it
Agents vote on pre-written candidate answers without generating arguments
Each agent proposes a solution, other agents challenge it, and over several rounds weaker arguments are exposed and a final answer is selected or synthesized
questions.explanation: In MAD, agents propose solutions or viewpoints, critique each other over rounds so stronger arguments are refined, and a final answer is then selected or synthesized. It is an argumentation process, not task decomposition or silent rewriting.

questions.stem: After a question is posed, which ordering matches the typical Multi-Agent Debate flow described in class?
options: Aggregation, then critique, then independent initial answers
Independent initial answers, then critique, then optional revision rounds, then aggregation
Critique of the question itself, then a single shared answer, then voting
Revision rounds first, then initial answers, then a judge's critique
questions.explanation: The typical flow is: multiple agents independently produce initial answers; agents critique each other's answers; optional revision rounds update the answers; and finally a judge or voting mechanism aggregates the result.

questions.stem: In a Multi-Agent Debate system, the judge/aggregator role is responsible for:
options: Selecting or synthesizing the final output from the debated answers
Generating the initial candidate answers to the question
Attacking weaknesses in the other agents' arguments
Executing unit tests on each agent's proposed solution
questions.explanation: Proposers generate initial answers and critics attack weaknesses; the judge/aggregator selects or synthesizes the final output. Proposer and critic can even be the same debate agent, but the judge role is distinct.

questions.stem: In the phishing-email MAD example, one agent argues the "affirmative" and another the "negative" while a judge produces the final classification. What governs rules such as whether the agents take turns or generate their arguments in parallel?
options: The judge's private system prompt
The shared blackboard's locking policy
An interaction protocol that defines the rules of the debate
A temperature schedule applied across rounds
questions.explanation: An interaction protocol governs the debate's rules — for example, turn-taking versus parallel argument generation — while the judge manages the process and evaluates the arguments to produce the final classification.

questions.stem: In the MAD study on code summarization, the judge agent is asked to evaluate debater responses using which criteria?
options: BLEU, ROUGE, and SIDE similarity scores against a reference summary
Expressiveness, content adequacy, and conciseness
Correctness, branch coverage, and execution speed
Expressiveness, attack rate, and output accuracy
questions.explanation: The judge scores responses on expressiveness (clarity and readability), content adequacy (coverage of key details), and conciseness (avoiding unnecessary detail). SIDE is an evaluation metric used by the researchers, not a criterion the judge is prompted with.

questions.stem: What did the paper "Is Multi-Agent Debate the Silver Bullet?" conclude about applying default MAD to software-engineering tasks?
options: MAD outperformed the ASCS baseline on every textual-similarity metric for summarization
MAD excelled at code translation but added nothing for code summarization
MAD only helped when the number of debaters exceeded ten
On the SIDE metric of code-summary semantic alignment MAD significantly beat ASCS, and MAD worked for summarization but showed limited performance on code translation
questions.explanation: ASCS generally matched or beat MAD on similarity-to-human-summary metrics, but on SIDE — which measures semantic alignment between code and summary — default MAD significantly outperformed ASCS; MAD transferred well to summarization but not to translation.

questions.stem: You adapt MAD to classify requirements as functional or non-functional, following the design shown in class. Which setup matches that architecture?
options: One debater argues the requirement is functional, another argues it is non-functional, and a judge decides who is right
Both debaters argue for the same label and a judge checks their reasoning for contradictions
Ten classifier agents each output a label and the majority label wins with no debate
A chain where an analyst labels the requirement and a tester validates the label
questions.explanation: The class design assigns adversarial roles: a functional debater argues why the requirement is functional, a non-functional debater argues the opposite, and a judge picks the winner. This MAD setup beat the baseline of simply asking the LLM for the label.

questions.stem: One extension to MAD mentioned in class aims to increase evidence diversification. How does it work?
options: Each debater runs at a different temperature to diversify its writing style
The number of revision rounds is increased until the debaters converge
Different agents are given different tools or data sources — e.g., one gets Internet search while another gets a RAG tool over a specific data source
Each debater is fine-tuned on a different slice of the training data
questions.explanation: The extension equips agents with different tools and/or access to different data sources, so the debate draws on genuinely diverse evidence rather than just stylistic variation. This directly supports MAD's premise that diverse viewpoints improve the solution.

questions.stem: Recitation 12 stresses that adding five agents instead of one does not automatically yield a better system. What primarily defines a multi-agent system, according to its key idea?
options: The total number of tokens the agents exchange per task
The design of the communication structure among the agents, not just the number of agents
The size of the largest model used by any single agent
The number of distinct tools available across all agents
questions.explanation: The recitation's key idea is that multi-agent systems are about designing the communication structure — who talks to whom, who decides, who verifies — rather than merely adding more agents.

questions.stem: In a chain pattern Requirement Analyst → Architect → Developer → Tester, the analyst misunderstands the task. Per Recitation 12, what is the likely outcome?
options: The architect catches the error, because each chain agent re-validates its predecessor's work
The system halts immediately, since chains cannot proceed past an incorrect step
An orchestrator reroutes the task to a different analyst automatically
The mistake propagates: the architect designs the wrong system, the developer builds the wrong thing, and the tester may only check that the wrong thing works
questions.explanation: A key chain weakness is that early mistakes propagate downstream — later agents tend to blindly trust earlier outputs and verification comes too late. There is no orchestrator in a pure chain, and agents do not re-validate upstream work by default.

questions.stem: In the star / hub-and-spoke pattern, which set of decisions belongs to the central controller?
options: Which specialist to call, what task to give it, how to combine outputs, and when to stop
Writing the code for every subtask itself and using specialists only for review
Verifying every worker's output by running integration tests
Broadcasting each incoming message to all specialists simultaneously
questions.explanation: The star's controller decides which specialist to call, what task to assign, how to combine their outputs, and when to stop. Broadcasting to everyone is closer to a mesh, and doing the work itself defeats the routing purpose of the hub.

questions.stem: You must build a system that brainstorms 20 creative product ideas and critiques them from many different perspectives, with no obvious ordering of steps. Which orchestration pattern from Recitation 12 fits best?
options: Chain / waterfall
Workflow graph with approval gates
Mesh / swarm
Hierarchical supervisor-worker with verification
questions.explanation: Mesh/swarm shines for open-ended tasks needing many perspectives, brainstorming, and debate with no obvious order. Chains need known phases, and workflow graphs or hierarchical verification target reliability rather than creative diversity.

questions.stem: A free-form swarm of agents discusses an ambiguous bug. Over many rounds the agents grow more confident while converging on the same wrong diagnosis, and the discussion shows no sign of ending. Which mesh/swarm weaknesses does this illustrate?
options: Controller bottleneck and single point of failure
Agents reinforcing each other's mistakes, plus the risk of endless discussion
Verification arriving too late in a fixed sequence of phases
A poor scoring mechanism selecting the wrong team of specialists
questions.explanation: Mesh/swarm systems risk agents reinforcing each other's mistakes and endless discussion, and they are harder to control and debug. Controller bottlenecks belong to star topologies, late verification to chains, and team-scoring failures to dynamic graphs.

questions.stem: An agent must solve a very long, structured puzzle in which a single wrong step ruins the final answer, and the task decomposes naturally into small units. Which pattern does Recitation 12 point to for this case?
options: Mesh / swarm, so many agents can explore the puzzle freely
Chain / waterfall, since the puzzle steps have a natural order
Dynamic graphs, so the system can drop agents that stop being useful
Hierarchical supervisor-worker with verification, so each small subtask is checked before errors can compound
questions.explanation: Hierarchical supervisor-worker with verification suits long tasks where errors compound and each small step must be correct: the supervisor decomposes, workers solve subtasks, and verifiers check them. A plain chain would let a single early mistake propagate to the end.

questions.stem: A company wants agents to answer customer-support tickets, but any refund must be approved by a human, and the system needs production-grade reliability with retries and observability. Which pattern from Recitation 12 is the BEST fit?
options: A workflow graph (flow engineering) — an explicit state machine with guard nodes, checkpoints, and approval gates, e.g. built with LangGraph
A mesh/swarm so agents can debate whether each refund is justified
A pure chain, adding the human as the last agent in the sequence
A dynamic graph that decides at runtime whether a human is needed
questions.explanation: Workflow graphs are recommended for production: an explicit state machine whose nodes are work steps and whose edges are conditional transitions supports guard nodes, checkpoints, human approval gates, retries, and observability — exactly what a human-approved refund flow needs. LangGraph is the example framework.

questions.stem: An agent platform offers 30 possible expert agents, but a typical user query only needs about 3 of them. Why does Recitation 12 favor a dynamic-graph pattern here rather than always invoking all 30 experts?
options: A star controller cannot technically connect to more than a handful of spokes
Invoking all experts would force a shared blackboard, which cannot hold 30 outputs
Dynamic graphs select the needed team at runtime, reducing unnecessary agent calls and balancing performance against cost
Dynamic graphs entirely eliminate the risk of bad routing decisions
questions.explanation: Dynamic graphs decide at runtime which agents are needed and which are not, cutting unnecessary calls and balancing performance with cost — calling all 30 every time would be wasteful. They do NOT remove routing risk; in fact they require a good scoring or routing mechanism to work.
